---
title: 'AI News Audit: How AI Models Use and\newline Distribute Canadian Journalism'
short_title: "AI News Audit"
series: "MTD/MEO Technical Brief"
date: "March 2026"
output:
  pdf_document:
    template: template.tex
    latex_engine: xelatex
    pandoc_args:
      - "--lua-filter=fullwidth-figures.lua"
    keep_tex: false
---

<!--
  MEO Ecosystem Brief — AI News Audit (merged figures + prose)
  Renders via: Rscript -e 'rmarkdown::render("brief.Rmd")'
  Figures generated inline by R chunks, saved to analysis/figures/ via save_plot().
  Prose numbers computed from data via inline R expressions.
-->

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
  cache = TRUE, cache.path = "analysis/cache/",
  fig.path = "analysis/figures/",
  fig.show = "hide"
)

# ── Auto-numbering for figures ──
# Define figure labels here IN DOCUMENT ORDER. To reorder figures, just
# reorder this vector — all fig_ref() and fig_num() calls update automatically.
.fig_labels <- c("methodology", "knowledge", "reproduction", "attribution", "concentration")
.fig_map <- setNames(seq_along(.fig_labels), .fig_labels)

# fig_num("label") — call at each figure definition (returns its number)
# fig_ref("label") — call in running text (same thing, just semantic alias)
fig_num <- function(label) {
  if (is.null(.fig_map[label])) stop("Unknown figure label: ", label)
  .fig_map[[label]]
}
fig_ref <- fig_num
```

```{r libraries, cache=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(jsonlite)
library(scales)
library(ggimage)
library(magick)
library(grid)
# library(packcircles)  # only needed for disabled circle-packing plot
library(cowplot)
library(irr)

source("analysis/meo_theme.R")
```

```{r theme-and-constants, cache=FALSE}
theme_brief <- theme_meo +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, hjust = 0),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

agent_colors <- c(
  "ChatGPT"  = "#467742",
  "Claude"   = "#434E7C",
  "Gemini"   = "#6BADC6",
  "Grok"     = "#FF8200"
)

agent_levels <- c("ChatGPT", "Claude", "Gemini", "Grok")

provider_to_agent <- c(
  "openai" = "ChatGPT", "anthropic" = "Claude",
  "gemini" = "Gemini", "xai" = "Grok"
)

clean_agent <- function(x) {
  case_when(
    str_detect(x, regex("gpt|openai|chatgpt", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "ChatGPT",
    str_detect(x, regex("claude|anthropic", ignore_case = TRUE))    ~ "Claude",
    str_detect(x, regex("gemini", ignore_case = TRUE))              ~ "Gemini",
    str_detect(x, regex("grok|xai", ignore_case = TRUE))            ~ "Grok",
    TRUE ~ x
  )
}

logo_dir <- "analysis/figures/logos"
agent_logos <- c(
  "ChatGPT" = file.path(logo_dir, "chatgpt.png"),
  "Claude"  = file.path(logo_dir, "claude.png"),
  "Gemini"  = file.path(logo_dir, "gemini.png"),
  "Grok"    = file.path(logo_dir, "grok.png")
)

cutoffs <- tibble(
  agent_clean = factor(agent_levels, levels = agent_levels),
  cutoff_date = as.Date(c("2024-05-31", "2025-02-28", "2025-01-31", "2024-11-30"))
)

cutoff_lookup <- setNames(cutoffs$cutoff_date, as.character(cutoffs$agent_clean))

# Flagship model cutoffs (GPT-5.2, Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3 Pro, Grok 4)
# Source: query_flagship.py MODELS dict (authoritative)
flagship_cutoffs <- tibble(
  agent_clean = factor(agent_levels, levels = agent_levels),
  cutoff_date = as.Date(c("2025-08-31", "2025-03-01", "2025-01-31", "2024-11-30"))
)
flagship_cutoff_lookup <- setNames(flagship_cutoffs$cutoff_date, as.character(flagship_cutoffs$agent_clean))

cdn_news_outlets <- c("CBC News", "CBC", "The Globe and Mail", "Globe and Mail",
                      "CTV News", "CTV", "Global News", "National Post",
                      "Toronto Star", "The Toronto Star",
                      "La Presse", "Radio-Canada", "BNN Bloomberg",
                      "The Canadian Press", "Montreal Gazette", "The Montreal Gazette",
                      "Maclean's",
                      "Financial Post", "City News", "Yahoo News Canada",
                      "TVA Nouvelles", "TVA", "Journal de Montréal",
                      "Journal de Montreal", "Le Devoir", "Noovo Info",
                      "Journal de Quebec", "Le Journal de Québec", "RDI", "LCN",
                      "Le Droit", "Le Nouvelliste", "Le Quotidien")

# Domain count for methodology text (must match CDN_NEWS_DOMAINS in scripts/code_responses.py)
cdn_news_domain_list <- 76L
```

```{r load-data, cache=TRUE, cache.extra=tools::md5sum(c("data/track1_coded.jsonl", "data/track1_prompts.jsonl", "data/track1_flagship_coded.jsonl", "data/track2_deterministic.csv", "data/track2_coded.csv", "data/track2_responses.jsonl", "data/track2_flagship_pilot.csv", "data/track1_coded_gpt_fr.jsonl", "data/track1_prompts_fr.jsonl", "data/track1_flagship_coded_gpt_fr.jsonl", "data/track1_coded_qwen.jsonl", "data/track1_coded_qwen_fr.jsonl", "data/track1_flagship_coded_qwen.jsonl", "data/track1_flagship_coded_qwen_fr.jsonl", "data/track2_coded_qwen.jsonl"))}
# Track 1: LLM-coded citation audit (no web search)
t1 <- stream_in(file("data/track1_coded.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE) |>
  mutate(
    agent_clean = recode(provider, !!!provider_to_agent),
    agent_clean = factor(agent_clean, levels = agent_levels),
    cites_canadian = canadian_sources_cited > 0,
    cites_anything = citation_type != "none",
    knows_story = knowledge_level %in% c("knowledgeable", "partial")
  )

t1_prompts <- stream_in(file("data/track1_prompts.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE)
t1 <- t1 |> left_join(t1_prompts |> select(story_id, n_outlets, engagement, date), by = "story_id")

# Track 2: deterministic-coded content theft probe (web search enabled)
t2 <- read_csv("data/track2_deterministic.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  distinct(article_id, agent, framing, citation, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    agent_clean = clean_agent(agent),
    agent_clean = factor(agent_clean, levels = agent_levels),
    cited = source_cited != "none",
    paywall_label = if_else(paywall == "True" | paywall == TRUE, "Paywalled", "Free"),
    framing_label = factor(framing_label, levels = c("Generic", "Specific", "Direct")),
    citation_label = factor(citation_label, levels = c("Unprompted", "Cite sources"))
  )

# Track 1 flagship: LLM-coded citation audit (flagship models, no web search)
t1_flag <- stream_in(file("data/track1_flagship_coded.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE) |>
  mutate(
    agent_clean = recode(provider, !!!provider_to_agent),
    agent_clean = factor(agent_clean, levels = agent_levels),
    cites_canadian = canadian_sources_cited > 0,
    cites_anything = citation_type != "none",
    knows_story = knowledge_level %in% c("knowledgeable", "partial")
  )

t1_flag <- t1_flag |> left_join(t1_prompts |> select(story_id, n_outlets, engagement, date), by = "story_id")

# Track 1 FR: French-language stories (economy)
t1_fr_prompts <- stream_in(file("data/track1_prompts_fr.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE)

t1_fr <- stream_in(file("data/track1_coded_gpt_fr.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE) |>
  filter(!is.na(citation_type)) |>
  mutate(
    agent_clean = recode(provider, !!!provider_to_agent),
    agent_clean = factor(agent_clean, levels = agent_levels),
    cites_canadian = canadian_sources_cited > 0,
    cites_anything = citation_type != "none",
    knows_story = knowledge_level %in% c("knowledgeable", "partial"),
    lang = "fr"
  ) |>
  left_join(t1_fr_prompts |> select(story_id, n_outlets, engagement, date), by = "story_id")

t1_fr_flag <- stream_in(file("data/track1_flagship_coded_gpt_fr.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE) |>
  mutate(
    agent_clean = recode(provider, !!!provider_to_agent),
    agent_clean = factor(agent_clean, levels = agent_levels),
    cites_canadian = canadian_sources_cited > 0,
    cites_anything = citation_type != "none",
    knows_story = knowledge_level %in% c("knowledgeable", "partial"),
    lang = "fr"
  ) |>
  left_join(t1_fr_prompts |> select(story_id, n_outlets, engagement, date), by = "story_id")

# Tag EN data with lang for combined analyses
t1      <- t1      |> mutate(lang = "en")
t1_flag <- t1_flag |> mutate(lang = "en")

# Join LLM-coded reproduction from track2_coded.csv
t2_llm <- read_csv("data/track2_coded.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  select(article_id, agent, framing, citation, llm_reproduction, llm_accuracy, llm_canadian_sources) |>
  distinct(article_id, agent, framing, citation, .keep_all = TRUE)
t2 <- t2 |> left_join(t2_llm, by = c("article_id", "agent", "framing", "citation"))

# has_cdn_url: TRUE if response contains a URL to a Canadian news domain
# (76 domains including cbc.ca, thestar.com, ca.news.yahoo.com, etc.)
# in response text or API citations. Coded by scripts/code_responses.py.
# Note: has_url (any URL) is distinct from has_cdn_url (Canadian news URL only).
t2 <- t2 |> mutate(has_cdn_url = if_else(
  has_cdn_url == "True" | has_cdn_url == TRUE, TRUE, FALSE
))

# t2_url: subset with verified responses available in JSONL.
# With full 3,360 responses recovered, t2_url should equal t2.
t2_responses <- stream_in(file("data/track2_responses.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE)
t2_response_keys <- t2_responses |>
  distinct(article_id, agent, framing, citation, tier) |>
  mutate(has_response = TRUE)
t2 <- t2 |> left_join(t2_response_keys, by = c("article_id", "agent", "framing", "citation"))
t2$tier <- factor(coalesce(t2$tier, "economy"), levels = c("economy", "flagship"))
t2_url <- t2 |> filter(has_response == TRUE)
```

```{r intercoder-reliability, cache=TRUE, dependson="load-data"}
# ── Intercoder reliability: GPT-5.2 vs Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-FP8 ──
# Quadratic-weighted kappa (κ_w) for ordinal dimensions

# Helper: compute weighted kappa from two factor vectors (same levels, ordinal)
wkappa <- function(x, y, levels) {
  x <- factor(x, levels = levels)
  y <- factor(y, levels = levels)
  complete <- !is.na(x) & !is.na(y)
  if (sum(complete) < 10) return(NA_real_)
  kappa2(cbind(as.integer(x[complete]), as.integer(y[complete])), weight = "squared")$value
}

# ── Load all Qwen-coded data ──
# Track 1 EN economy
q1 <- stream_in(file("data/track1_coded_qwen.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE)
q1_flat <- q1 |>
  mutate(
    q_knowledge    = coding$knowledge_level,
    q_citation     = coding$citation_type,
    q_accuracy     = coding$accuracy,
    q_cdn_sources  = coding$canadian_sources_cited
  ) |>
  select(story_id, provider, q_knowledge, q_citation, q_accuracy, q_cdn_sources)

# Track 1 EN flagship
q1f <- stream_in(file("data/track1_flagship_coded_qwen.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE)
q1f_flat <- q1f |>
  mutate(
    q_knowledge    = coding$knowledge_level,
    q_citation     = coding$citation_type,
    q_accuracy     = coding$accuracy,
    q_cdn_sources  = coding$canadian_sources_cited
  ) |>
  select(story_id, provider, q_knowledge, q_citation, q_accuracy, q_cdn_sources)

# Track 1 FR economy
q1_fr <- stream_in(file("data/track1_coded_qwen_fr.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE)
q1_fr_flat <- q1_fr |>
  mutate(
    q_knowledge    = coding$knowledge_level,
    q_citation     = coding$citation_type,
    q_accuracy     = coding$accuracy,
    q_cdn_sources  = coding$canadian_sources_cited
  ) |>
  select(story_id, provider, q_knowledge, q_citation, q_accuracy, q_cdn_sources)

# Track 1 FR flagship
q1f_fr <- stream_in(file("data/track1_flagship_coded_qwen_fr.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE)
q1f_fr_flat <- q1f_fr |>
  mutate(
    q_knowledge    = coding$knowledge_level,
    q_citation     = coding$citation_type,
    q_accuracy     = coding$accuracy,
    q_cdn_sources  = coding$canadian_sources_cited
  ) |>
  select(story_id, provider, q_knowledge, q_citation, q_accuracy, q_cdn_sources)

# Track 2 Qwen
q2 <- stream_in(file("data/track2_coded_qwen.jsonl"), verbose = FALSE) |>
  filter(!is.na(coding$reproduction_level), is.na(error) | error == "") |>
  mutate(
    parts = str_split(custom_id, "__"),
    article_id = map_chr(parts, 1),
    agent      = map_chr(parts, 2),
    framing    = map_chr(parts, 3),
    citation   = map_chr(parts, 4),
    q_reproduction   = coding$reproduction_level,
    q_attribution    = coding$attribution_level,
    q_accuracy       = coding$factual_accuracy,
    q_paywall        = coding$paywalled_content_reproduced,
    q_cdn_sources    = coding$canadian_sources_count
  ) |>
  select(article_id, agent, framing, citation,
         q_reproduction, q_attribution, q_accuracy, q_paywall, q_cdn_sources)

# ── Join GPT and Qwen for each corpus ──

# Track 1 EN economy
icr_t1 <- t1 |>
  select(story_id, provider, knowledge_level, citation_type, accuracy, canadian_sources_cited) |>
  inner_join(q1_flat, by = c("story_id", "provider"))

# Track 1 EN flagship
icr_t1f <- t1_flag |>
  select(story_id, provider, knowledge_level, citation_type, accuracy, canadian_sources_cited) |>
  inner_join(q1f_flat, by = c("story_id", "provider"))

# Track 1 FR economy
icr_t1_fr <- t1_fr |>
  select(story_id, provider, knowledge_level, citation_type, accuracy, canadian_sources_cited) |>
  inner_join(q1_fr_flat, by = c("story_id", "provider"))

# Track 1 FR flagship
icr_t1f_fr <- t1_fr_flag |>
  select(story_id, provider, knowledge_level, citation_type, accuracy, canadian_sources_cited) |>
  inner_join(q1f_fr_flat, by = c("story_id", "provider"))

# Track 2
icr_t2 <- t2 |>
  select(article_id, agent, framing, citation, llm_reproduction, llm_accuracy) |>
  inner_join(q2, by = c("article_id", "agent", "framing", "citation"))

# ── Compute kappas (pooled across all corpora) ──
knowledge_levels <- c("refusal", "no_knowledge", "partial", "knowledgeable")
citation_levels  <- c("none", "vague_reference", "recommended", "named_as_source")
accuracy_levels  <- c("unverifiable", "inaccurate", "mostly_accurate", "accurate")
repro_levels     <- c("none", "topic_only", "partial", "close_paraphrase", "verbatim")
attrib_levels    <- c("none", "misattribution", "vague", "outlet_named", "full")

# Pool all Track 1 corpora (EN+FR, economy+flagship) for single pooled kappas
icr_t1_pooled <- bind_rows(icr_t1, icr_t1f, icr_t1_fr, icr_t1f_fr)

kw_t1_knowledge  <- wkappa(icr_t1_pooled$knowledge_level, icr_t1_pooled$q_knowledge, knowledge_levels)
kw_t1_citation   <- wkappa(icr_t1_pooled$citation_type, icr_t1_pooled$q_citation, citation_levels)
kw_t1_accuracy   <- wkappa(icr_t1_pooled$accuracy, icr_t1_pooled$q_accuracy, accuracy_levels)
kw_t1_cdn        <- wkappa(
  pmin(icr_t1_pooled$canadian_sources_cited, 5),
  pmin(icr_t1_pooled$q_cdn_sources, 5),
  0:5
)

# Track 2 kappas (single corpus)
kw_t2_repro <- wkappa(icr_t2$llm_reproduction, icr_t2$q_reproduction, repro_levels)
kw_t2_accuracy <- wkappa(icr_t2$llm_accuracy, icr_t2$q_accuracy, accuracy_levels)
kw_t2_paywall <- wkappa(
  as.character(icr_t2$llm_reproduction %in% c("verbatim", "close_paraphrase", "partial")),
  as.character(icr_t2$q_reproduction %in% c("verbatim", "close_paraphrase", "partial")),
  c("FALSE", "TRUE")
)

# ── Formatted strings for inline text ──
kw_all_knowledge <- sprintf("%.2f", kw_t1_knowledge)
kw_all_citation  <- sprintf("%.2f", kw_t1_citation)
kw_all_accuracy  <- sprintf("%.2f", kw_t1_accuracy)
kw_all_cdn       <- sprintf("%.2f", kw_t1_cdn)

# Total Qwen-coded responses
n_qwen_t1 <- nrow(icr_t1_pooled)
n_qwen_t2 <- nrow(icr_t2)
```

```{r summary-stats, cache=TRUE, dependson="load-data"}
# ── Track 1 totals ──
n_stories <- n_distinct(t1$story_id)
n_stories_fr <- n_distinct(t1_fr$story_id)
n_stories_total <- n_stories + n_stories_fr
n_t1      <- nrow(t1)

# ── Track 1: Canadian citation rate by agent ──
agent_cdn_pct <- t1 |>
  group_by(agent_clean) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(cites_canadian) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
  deframe()

# ── Track 1: Knowledge accuracy for training-data stories ──
t1_training <- t1 |>
  mutate(
    story_date = as.Date(date),
    agent_cutoff = cutoff_lookup[as.character(agent_clean)],
    in_training = story_date <= agent_cutoff,
    accurate = knows_story & accuracy %in% c("accurate", "mostly_accurate"),
    knowledge_cat = case_when(
      knows_story & accuracy %in% c("accurate", "mostly_accurate") ~ "Accurate",
      knows_story ~ "Inaccurate",
      TRUE ~ "No knowledge"
    )
  )

# % that "know" the story (accurate + inaccurate) for training-data stories
knows_training_pct <- t1_training |>
  filter(in_training) |>
  group_by(agent_clean) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(knows_story) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
  deframe()

# % accurate for training-data stories
accurate_training_pct <- t1_training |>
  filter(in_training) |>
  group_by(agent_clean) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(accurate) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
  deframe()

# % of pre-training responses with citation_type == "none" (no attribution at all)
# Pool both economy and flagship tiers for this stat
t1_flag_training <- t1_flag |>
  mutate(
    story_date = as.Date(date),
    agent_cutoff = flagship_cutoff_lookup[as.character(agent_clean)],
    in_training = story_date <= agent_cutoff
  )
# pct_no_cite_training computed below after t1_fr_training is defined

# Claude "no knowledge" rate for training-data stories
claude_no_knowledge_pct <- t1_training |>
  filter(in_training, agent_clean == "Claude") |>
  summarise(pct = mean(knowledge_cat == "No knowledge") * 100) |>
  pull(pct)

# ── Post-cutoff hallucination stats (pooled EN+FR, for footnote) ──
post_hallucination <- function(data, cutoff_map) {
  data |>
    mutate(
      story_date    = as.Date(date),
      agent_cutoff  = as.Date(cutoff_map[as.character(agent_clean)]),
      in_training   = story_date <= agent_cutoff,
      claims        = knowledge_level %in% c("knowledgeable", "partial"),
      inaccurate    = !accuracy %in% c("accurate", "mostly_accurate")
    ) |>
    filter(!in_training) |>
    group_by(agent_clean) |>
    summarise(
      pct_claims    = round(mean(claims) * 100),
      pct_wrong     = round(mean(inaccurate[claims]) * 100),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
}
hall_econ <- post_hallucination(bind_rows(t1, t1_fr),           cutoff_lookup)
hall_flag <- post_hallucination(bind_rows(t1_flag, t1_fr_flag), flagship_cutoff_lookup)
hall <- function(agent, tier, col) {
  d <- if (tier == "econ") hall_econ else hall_flag
  d[[col]][d$agent_clean == agent]
}

# ── Track 2 totals ──
n_articles <- n_distinct(t2$article_id)
n_t2       <- nrow(t2)

# ── Track 2: reproduction vs citation gaps (all conditions) ──
t2_gaps <- t2 |>
  mutate(reproduces = llm_reproduction %in% c("partial", "close_paraphrase", "verbatim")) |>
  group_by(agent_clean) |>
  summarise(
    pct_repro = mean(reproduces) * 100,
    pct_cite = mean(cited) * 100,
    gap = mean(reproduces) * 100 - mean(cited) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )
gap_range <- range(t2_gaps$gap)

# ── Track 2: generic unprompted reproduction rates (for Fig 2) ──
# Uses LLM-coded reproduction (viable summary = partial + close_paraphrase + verbatim)
t2_generic <- t2_url |>
  filter(framing_label == "Generic", citation_label == "Unprompted") |>
  mutate(
    reproduces = llm_reproduction %in% c("partial", "close_paraphrase", "verbatim")
  ) |>
  group_by(agent_clean) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    pct_repro = mean(reproduces) * 100,
    pct_cite = mean(cited) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )
pw_generic_repro <- setNames(t2_generic$pct_repro, as.character(t2_generic$agent_clean))
n_generic_unprompted <- sum(t2_generic$n)

# ── Track 2: reproduction level breakdown (generic unprompted) ──
repro_breakdown <- t2_url |>
  filter(framing_label == "Generic", citation_label == "Unprompted") |>
  count(llm_reproduction) |>
  mutate(pct = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1))
pct_partial <- repro_breakdown$pct[repro_breakdown$llm_reproduction == "partial"]
pct_close   <- repro_breakdown$pct[repro_breakdown$llm_reproduction == "close_paraphrase"]
pct_verbatim <- repro_breakdown$pct[repro_breakdown$llm_reproduction == "verbatim"]

# ── Track 1: outlet citation counts (economy + flagship, EN + FR) ──
outlet_counts <- bind_rows(t1, t1_flag, t1_fr, t1_fr_flag) |>
  filter(map_lgl(sources_cited, ~ length(.x) > 0)) |>
  unnest(sources_cited) |>
  rename(source = sources_cited) |>
  filter(source %in% cdn_news_outlets) |>
  mutate(source = case_when(
    source %in% c("CTV", "CTV News") ~ "CTV News",
    source %in% c("CBC", "CBC News") ~ "CBC News",
    source %in% c("Globe and Mail", "The Globe and Mail") ~ "The Globe and Mail",
    source %in% c("Toronto Star", "The Toronto Star") ~ "Toronto Star",
    source %in% c("Montreal Gazette", "The Montreal Gazette") ~ "Montreal Gazette",
    TRUE ~ source
  )) |>
  count(source, sort = TRUE)

# Helper to look up outlet citation count
outlet_n <- function(name) {
  val <- outlet_counts$n[outlet_counts$source == name]
  if (length(val) == 0) 0L else val
}

# ── Track 2: clickable pathway stats (verified responses only) ──
t2_pathway <- t2_url |>
  mutate(
    names_outlet = source_cited != "none",
    names_any_cdn = !is.na(llm_canadian_sources) & llm_canadian_sources > 0,
    has_cdn_url_bool = has_cdn_url
  ) |>
  group_by(agent_clean, framing_label, citation_label) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    pct_names = mean(names_outlet) * 100,
    pct_names_any_cdn = mean(names_any_cdn) * 100,
    pct_cdn_url = mean(has_cdn_url_bool) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Generic + Unprompted naming rates
pw_generic <- t2_pathway |> filter(framing_label == "Generic", citation_label == "Unprompted")
pw_generic_names <- setNames(pw_generic$pct_names, as.character(pw_generic$agent_clean))
pw_generic_names_any_cdn <- setNames(pw_generic$pct_names_any_cdn, as.character(pw_generic$agent_clean))
pw_generic_urls <- setNames(pw_generic$pct_cdn_url, as.character(pw_generic$agent_clean))

# Direct + Cite sources naming/URL rates (most favourable conditions)
pw_direct <- t2_pathway |> filter(framing_label == "Direct", citation_label == "Cite sources")
pw_direct_names <- setNames(pw_direct$pct_names, as.character(pw_direct$agent_clean))
pw_direct_urls <- setNames(pw_direct$pct_cdn_url, as.character(pw_direct$agent_clean))

# Claude citation multiplier over ChatGPT
claude_cite_ratio <- round(agent_cdn_pct["Claude"] / agent_cdn_pct["ChatGPT"], 0)

# ── Track 2: Paywall vs free reproduction rates (for inline text) ──
# Filter to Generic + Unprompted to match Figure 2 conditions
t2_pw_rates <- t2_url |>
  filter(framing_label == "Generic", citation_label == "Unprompted") |>
  mutate(
    reproduces = llm_reproduction %in% c("partial", "close_paraphrase", "verbatim"),
    pw = if_else(paywall == "True" | paywall == TRUE, "Paywalled", "Free")
  ) |>
  group_by(pw) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(reproduces) * 100, n = n(), .groups = "drop")
pw_free_repro_pct <- t2_pw_rates$pct[t2_pw_rates$pw == "Free"]
pw_paid_repro_pct <- t2_pw_rates$pct[t2_pw_rates$pw == "Paywalled"]
pw_free_n <- t2_pw_rates$n[t2_pw_rates$pw == "Free"]
pw_paid_n <- t2_pw_rates$n[t2_pw_rates$pw == "Paywalled"]

# Outlet-level reproduction rates (Generic/Unprompted)
t2_outlet_rates <- t2_url |>
  filter(framing_label == "Generic", citation_label == "Unprompted") |>
  mutate(reproduces = llm_reproduction %in% c("partial", "close_paraphrase", "verbatim")) |>
  group_by(source) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(reproduces) * 100, n = n(), .groups = "drop")
pw_logic_repro_pct <- t2_outlet_rates$pct[t2_outlet_rates$source == "The Logic"]
pw_star_repro_pct  <- t2_outlet_rates$pct[t2_outlet_rates$source == "The Toronto Star"]
pw_gazette_repro_pct <- t2_outlet_rates$pct[t2_outlet_rates$source == "The Gazette (Montreal)"]

# ── Track 2: accuracy of responses that cover distinctive reporting ──
t2_accuracy_overall <- t2 |>
  mutate(reproduces = llm_reproduction %in% c("partial", "close_paraphrase", "verbatim")) |>
  filter(reproduces) |>
  summarise(
    pct_accurate = mean(llm_accuracy %in% c("accurate", "mostly_accurate")) * 100,
    pct_inaccurate = mean(llm_accuracy == "inaccurate") * 100,
    n = n()
  )
t2_repro_pct_inaccurate <- t2_accuracy_overall$pct_inaccurate
t2_repro_pct_accurate <- t2_accuracy_overall$pct_accurate
t2_repro_n <- t2_accuracy_overall$n

# ── Track 2 flagship pilot: load from data_snafu/ backup ──
# 407 flagship-tier responses recovered from batch coding inputs.
# Not used in main analyses; robustness check only.
t2_flag_backup <- "data/track2_flagship_pilot.csv"
if (file.exists(t2_flag_backup)) {
  t2_flag_det <- read_csv(t2_flag_backup, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    filter(tier == "flagship") |>
    distinct(article_id, agent, framing, citation, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
    mutate(
      agent_clean = clean_agent(agent),
      agent_clean = factor(agent_clean, levels = agent_levels),
      cited = source_cited != "none",
      has_cdn_url = has_url,
      framing_label = factor(framing_label, levels = c("Generic", "Specific", "Direct")),
      citation_label = factor(citation_label, levels = c("Unprompted", "Cite sources"))
    )
} else {
  t2_flag_det <- tibble()
}

n_t2_flagship_pilot <- nrow(t2_flag_det)

# Helper for formatting p-values
fmt_p <- function(p) if (is.na(p)) "N/A" else format.pval(p, digits = 2, eps = 0.001)

if (n_t2_flagship_pilot > 0) {
  # Combine economy + flagship for t-tests
  t2_both <- bind_rows(
    t2 |> mutate(tier_test = "economy") |>
      select(article_id, agent_clean, framing_label, citation_label, cited, source_cited,
             has_cdn_url, llm_reproduction, paywall, tier_test),
    t2_flag_det |> mutate(tier_test = "flagship",
                           has_cdn_url = if_else(has_cdn_url == "True" | has_cdn_url == TRUE, TRUE, FALSE)) |>
      select(article_id, agent_clean, framing_label, citation_label, cited, source_cited,
             has_cdn_url, llm_reproduction, paywall, tier_test)
  ) |>
    mutate(
      reproduces_bin = as.integer(llm_reproduction %in% c("partial", "close_paraphrase", "verbatim")),
      names_outlet_bin = as.integer(source_cited != "none"),
      has_cdn_url_bin = as.integer(has_cdn_url),
      paywall_bin = paywall == "True" | paywall == TRUE
    )

  rb_repro   <- t.test(reproduces_bin ~ tier_test,
                       data = t2_both |> filter(framing_label == "Generic", citation_label == "Unprompted"))
  rb_naming  <- t.test(names_outlet_bin ~ tier_test,
                       data = t2_both |> filter(framing_label == "Direct", citation_label == "Cite sources"))
  rb_pw      <- t.test(reproduces_bin ~ tier_test,
                       data = t2_both |> filter(paywall_bin))
  rb_url_data <- t2_both |> filter(framing_label == "Direct", citation_label == "Cite sources",
                                    !is.na(has_cdn_url_bin))
  if (n_distinct(rb_url_data$tier_test) == 2 && sum(rb_url_data$tier_test == "flagship") >= 5) {
    rb_url <- t.test(has_cdn_url_bin ~ tier_test, data = rb_url_data)
  } else {
    rb_url <- list(p.value = NA_real_)
  }
  rb_ps <- na.omit(c(rb_repro$p.value, rb_naming$p.value, rb_pw$p.value, rb_url$p.value))
  rb_n_tests <- length(rb_ps)
  rb_ps_adj <- p.adjust(rb_ps, method = "holm")
} else {
  # No flagship data available — set placeholders
  rb_repro <- rb_naming <- rb_pw <- rb_url <- list(p.value = NA_real_, statistic = NA_real_, estimate = c(NA_real_, NA_real_))
  rb_ps <- numeric(0)
  rb_ps_adj <- numeric(0)
  rb_n_tests <- 0
}
rb_n_sig   <- sum(rb_ps < 0.05)
rb_n_sig_adj <- sum(rb_ps_adj < 0.05)

# ── FR citation stats (pre-cutoff stories only) ──
fr_cdn_outlets <- c("Radio-Canada", "La Presse", "TVA Nouvelles", "TVA",
                    "Journal de Montréal", "Journal de Montreal",
                    "Le Devoir", "Noovo Info", "Journal de Quebec",
                    "Le Journal de Québec", "RDI", "LCN")

t1_fr_training <- t1_fr |>
  mutate(
    story_date = as.Date(date),
    agent_cutoff = as.Date(cutoff_lookup[as.character(agent_clean)]),
    in_training = story_date <= agent_cutoff
  ) |>
  filter(in_training)

t1_fr_flag_training <- t1_fr_flag |>
  mutate(
    story_date = as.Date(date),
    agent_cutoff = as.Date(flagship_cutoff_lookup[as.character(agent_clean)]),
    in_training = story_date <= agent_cutoff
  ) |>
  filter(in_training)

pct_no_cite_fr_training <- bind_rows(
  t1_fr_training |> select(citation_type),
  t1_fr_flag_training |> select(citation_type)
) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(citation_type == "none") * 100) |>
  pull(pct)

pct_cites_fr_outlet_training <- bind_rows(
  t1_fr_training  |> select(sources_cited),
  t1_fr_flag_training |> select(sources_cited)
) |>
  mutate(cites_fr = map_lgl(sources_cited, ~ any(.x %in% fr_cdn_outlets))) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(cites_fr) * 100) |>
  pull(pct)

# % of pre-training responses with no citation (pooled EN + FR, economy + flagship)
pct_no_cite_training <- bind_rows(
  t1_training      |> filter(in_training) |> select(citation_type),
  t1_flag_training |> filter(in_training) |> select(citation_type),
  t1_fr_training                          |> select(citation_type),
  t1_fr_flag_training                     |> select(citation_type)
) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(citation_type == "none") * 100) |>
  pull(pct)

# % of pre-training responses with demonstrated knowledge AND no citation
# (the double condition: model knew the story but didn't attribute it)
pct_no_cite_knowing <- bind_rows(
  t1_training      |> filter(in_training) |> select(citation_type, knows_story),
  t1_flag_training |> filter(in_training) |> select(citation_type, knows_story),
  t1_fr_training                          |> select(citation_type, knows_story),
  t1_fr_flag_training                     |> select(citation_type, knows_story)
) |>
  filter(knows_story) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(citation_type == "none") * 100) |>
  pull(pct)

# % of pre-training responses that demonstrated knowledge (denominator for above)
pct_knowing_training <- bind_rows(
  t1_training      |> filter(in_training) |> select(knows_story),
  t1_flag_training |> filter(in_training) |> select(knows_story),
  t1_fr_training                          |> select(knows_story),
  t1_fr_flag_training                     |> select(knows_story)
) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(knows_story) * 100) |>
  pull(pct)

# EN-only comparison (for EN vs FR contrast)
pct_no_cite_en_training <- bind_rows(
  t1_training |> filter(in_training) |> select(citation_type, cites_canadian, agent_clean, lang),
  t1_flag_training |> filter(in_training) |> select(citation_type, cites_canadian, agent_clean, lang)
) |>
  summarise(pct = mean(citation_type == "none") * 100) |>
  pull(pct)

# ── Pooled EN + FR (pre-cutoff) ──
t1_pooled_training <- bind_rows(
  t1_training |> filter(in_training) |> select(citation_type, cites_canadian, agent_clean, lang),
  t1_flag_training |> filter(in_training) |> select(citation_type, cites_canadian, agent_clean, lang),
  t1_fr_training   |> select(citation_type, cites_canadian, agent_clean, lang),
  t1_fr_flag_training |> select(citation_type, cites_canadian, agent_clean, lang)
)
pct_no_cite_pooled <- mean(t1_pooled_training$citation_type == "none") * 100
pct_cdn_cite_pooled <- mean(t1_pooled_training$cites_canadian) * 100
```


::: bilingual

::: {.executive-summary}
## Executive summary

\vspace{6pt}

AI models have become a primary way for millions of Canadians to get information. When people ask ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, or Grok about current events, the responses they receive, and the sources those responses cite, shape which news organizations get traffic, attribution, and revenue. This brief presents the first large-scale empirical audit of how AI models use and distribute Canadian journalism.

\vspace{6pt}

We tested four major AI models on `r format(n_stories_total, big.mark = ",")` real Canadian news stories (English and French) without web search activated and found the same pattern across all of them. All four models showed extensive knowledge of Canadian current events consistent with having ingested Canadian news reporting. Models demonstrated at least partial knowledge in `r round(pct_knowing_training)`% of responses to stories within their training window, but among those knowledgeable responses, `r round(pct_no_cite_knowing)`% provided no source attribution of any kind.

\vspace{6pt}

When we enabled web search and tested `r n_articles` specific articles via each company's API, every model produced responses that covered enough of the original reporting that many consumers would rarely need to visit the source. Models often linked to Canadian news sites, with `r round(mean(unlist(pw_generic_urls)))`% of responses including at least one Canadian URL, but named a Canadian source in the response text only `r round(mean(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))`% of the time. Links provide a pathway back to the source, but consumers reading the response itself rarely see an indication of whose journalism they are consuming. 

\vspace{6pt}

AI models readily covered content from paywalled sources. In most cases, competitive news markets mean the same stories appear across multiple outlets, and models retrieve a free version. In some cases, API logs showed models citing paywalled URLs directly, suggesting that paywalls may not block automated retrieval the way they block human readers. Whether models access the content directly or through freely available alternatives, the result for publishers is the same: the originating outlet receives neither traffic nor credit. AI companies ingest Canadian journalism at scale, produce substitute content from it, and distribute that content to consumers. Links to Canadian news sites are provided, but the newsrooms that produced the journalism are rarely named in the response consumers read.

\vspace{6pt}

:::

::: {.executive-summary-fr}
## Résumé exécutif

\vspace{6pt}

Les modèles d'IA sont devenus l'un des principaux moyens de s'informer pour des millions de Canadiens. Lorsque des personnes interrogent ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude ou Grok sur l'actualité, les réponses qu'elles reçoivent — et les sources que ces réponses citent — influencent quels médias reçoivent du trafic, de l'attribution et des revenus. Ce rapport présente le premier audit empirique à grande échelle de la manière dont les modèles d'IA utilisent et distribuent le journalisme canadien.

\vspace{6pt}

Nous avons testé quatre modèles d'IA sur `r format(n_stories_total, big.mark = " ")` événements d'actualité canadiens réels (en anglais et en français) sans recherche web activée et avons observé le même schéma pour chacun d'eux. Les quatre modèles ont démontré une connaissance étendue de l'actualité canadienne, cohérente avec l'ingestion de reportages de médias canadiens — au moins une connaissance partielle dans `r round(pct_knowing_training)` % des réponses aux événements couverts par leurs données d'entraînement. Parmi ces réponses, `r round(pct_no_cite_knowing)` % ne fournissaient aucune attribution de source.

\vspace{6pt}

Lorsque nous avons activé la recherche web et testé `r n_articles` articles spécifiques via l'API de chaque entreprise, chaque modèle produisait des réponses couvrant suffisamment du reportage original pour que les consommateurs n'aient que rarement besoin de consulter la source. Les modèles incluaient souvent des liens vers des sites de médias canadiens, avec `r round(mean(unlist(pw_generic_urls)))` % des réponses contenant au moins une URL canadienne, mais ne nommaient une source canadienne dans le texte que `r round(mean(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))` % du temps. Les liens permettent de retracer la source, mais les consommateurs qui lisent la réponse elle-même voient rarement une indication du média dont ils consomment le journalisme.

\vspace{6pt}

Les modèles d'IA ont facilement couvert le contenu d'articles payants. Dans la plupart des cas, les marchés de l'information concurrentiels font que les mêmes articles paraissent dans plusieurs médias, et les modèles récupèrent une version gratuite. Dans certains cas, les journaux d'API indiquaient que les modèles citaient directement des URL payantes, ce qui suggère que les verrous d'accès payants ne bloquent pas nécessairement la récupération automatisée comme ils bloquent les lecteurs humains. Que les modèles accèdent au contenu directement ou via des alternatives gratuites, le résultat est le même pour les éditeurs : le média d'origine ne reçoit ni trafic ni crédit. Les entreprises d'IA ingèrent le journalisme canadien à grande échelle, produisent du contenu de substitution et le distribuent aux consommateurs. Des liens vers des sites de médias canadiens sont fournis, mais les salles de rédaction qui ont produit le journalisme sont rarement nommées dans la réponse que les consommateurs lisent.

\vspace{6pt}

:::

:::


::: bilingual

::: {.key-takeaways}
## Key Takeaways

\vspace{6pt}

- **AI models have absorbed Canadian news at scale and almost never say so.** All four models showed knowledge of Canadian current events for stories inside their training windows. Wire services and other public sources covering the same events may also contribute, but the pattern is consistent with having ingested Canadian news reporting. Among responses where a model demonstrated that knowledge, `r round(pct_no_cite_knowing)`% provided no source attribution of any kind: no named outlet, no recommendation, no reference.

- **AI models substitute for the article and link to sources, but rarely name them.** With web access, models covered enough distinctive reporting to reduce a consumer's need to visit the source in `r round(min(unlist(pw_generic_repro)))`% (`r names(which.min(unlist(pw_generic_repro)))`) to `r round(max(unlist(pw_generic_repro)))`% (`r names(which.max(unlist(pw_generic_repro)))`) of cases. Models linked to Canadian news sites in `r round(mean(unlist(pw_generic_urls)))`% of responses. But they rarely named any Canadian source in the response text: `r round(min(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))`% (`r names(which.min(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))`) to `r round(max(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))`% (`r names(which.max(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))`) of responses. When directly prompted, naming rates reached `r round(min(pw_direct_names))`% (`r names(which.min(pw_direct_names))`) to `r round(max(pw_direct_names))`% (`r names(which.max(pw_direct_names))`).

- **Paywalls do not protect content in a competitive news market.** AI models covered paywalled content (`r round(pw_paid_repro_pct)`%) at rates comparable to free content (`r round(pw_free_repro_pct)`%). For most stories, models find freely available versions elsewhere. In some cases, API logs showed models citing paywalled URLs directly with extensive verbatim reproduction, suggesting that paywalls may not block automated retrieval the way they block human readers. Either way, the result is the same: paywalled journalism is reproduced without compensation.

- **Naming sources is technically possible but rarely the default.** When prompted, naming rates reached `r round(min(pw_direct_names))`% (`r names(which.min(pw_direct_names))`) to `r round(max(pw_direct_names))`% (`r names(which.max(pw_direct_names))`). Under default conditions, models linked to Canadian news sites `r round(mean(unlist(pw_generic_urls)))`% of the time but rarely named the outlet in the response text itself. Links let a consumer reach the source; naming would tell them whose journalism they are reading. The gap between the two is a design choice.
:::

::: {.key-takeaways-fr}
## Principaux constats

\vspace{6pt}

- **Les modèles d'IA ont absorbé le journalisme canadien à grande échelle, et ne le disent presque jamais.** Les quatre modèles ont démontré une connaissance de l'actualité canadienne pour les sujets couverts par leurs données d'entraînement. Les agences de presse et d'autres sources publiques couvrant les mêmes événements peuvent aussi contribuer, mais le schéma est cohérent avec l'ingestion de reportages de médias canadiens. Parmi les réponses où un modèle a démontré cette connaissance, `r round(pct_no_cite_knowing)` % ne fournissaient aucune attribution : ni média nommé, ni recommandation, ni référence.

- **Les modèles d'IA se substituent à l'article et fournissent des liens, mais nomment rarement leurs sources.** Avec l'accès au web, les modèles couvraient suffisamment du reportage distinctif pour réduire le besoin de consulter la source dans `r round(min(unlist(pw_generic_repro)))` % (`r names(which.min(unlist(pw_generic_repro)))`) à `r round(max(unlist(pw_generic_repro)))` % (`r names(which.max(unlist(pw_generic_repro)))`) des cas. Les modèles incluaient des liens vers des sites de médias canadiens dans `r round(mean(unlist(pw_generic_urls)))` % des réponses. Mais ils nommaient rarement le média d'origine dans le texte : `r round(min(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))` % (`r names(which.min(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))`) à `r round(max(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))` % (`r names(which.max(unlist(pw_generic_names_any_cdn)))`) des réponses. Lorsque nous demandions des citations directement, les taux atteignaient `r round(min(pw_direct_names))` % (`r names(which.min(pw_direct_names))`) à `r round(max(pw_direct_names))` % (`r names(which.max(pw_direct_names))`).

- **Les verrous d'accès payants ne protègent pas le contenu dans un marché de l'information concurrentiel.** Les modèles d'IA ont couvert le contenu payant (`r round(pw_paid_repro_pct)` %) à des taux comparables au contenu gratuit (`r round(pw_free_repro_pct)` %). Pour la plupart des articles, les modèles trouvent des versions gratuites ailleurs. Dans certains cas, les journaux d'API ont montré des modèles citant directement des URL payantes avec une reproduction textuelle extensive, ce qui suggère que les verrous d'accès payants ne bloquent pas nécessairement la récupération automatisée comme ils bloquent les lecteurs humains. Dans tous les cas, le résultat est le même : le journalisme payant est reproduit sans compensation.

- **Nommer les sources est techniquement possible, mais rarement le comportement par défaut.** Lorsque nous demandions des citations, les taux de nomination atteignaient `r round(min(pw_direct_names))` % (`r names(which.min(pw_direct_names))`) à `r round(max(pw_direct_names))` % (`r names(which.max(pw_direct_names))`). Par défaut, les modèles incluaient des liens vers des médias canadiens `r round(mean(unlist(pw_generic_urls)))` % du temps, mais nommaient rarement le média dans le texte de la réponse. Les liens permettent au consommateur d'atteindre la source ; nommer le média lui dirait quel journalisme il lit. L'écart entre les deux est un choix de conception.
:::

:::

::: bilingual

::: {.why-it-matters}
## Why it Matters

\vspace{6pt}

Journalism is part of democratic infrastructure. It is essential for accountability, public debate, and informed civic participation. Journalism is also expensive to produce and has been under severe financial pressure for two decades. AI companies are now extracting value from it at every stage: ingesting news archives as training data, producing derivative content without naming the sources, and delivering answers to consumers that could reduce the need and incentive to visit the original source.

\vspace{6pt}

This is not a hypothetical concern. Our data show that AI models possess detailed knowledge of Canadian current events: knowledge that most plausibly comes from Canadian news reporting. They answer questions about Canadian politics, policy, and society with a specificity that points to Canadian journalism as the primary source. They often link to Canadian news sites, but they rarely name the journalists or organizations whose reporting informed the answer. A consumer who does not click through every link will not know whose journalism they just consumed. The result is a system in which AI companies monetize journalists' knowledge while the newsrooms that produced it receive links but not recognition. That dynamic, if sustained at scale, risks accelerating the decline of the journalism these models depend on.

\vspace{6pt}

An accompanying policy memo, "AI News Audit: AI, Canadian Journalism, and Paths for Policy Action", discusses the implications of these findings for Canadian copyright, the Online News Act, statutory licensing, and attribution standards.

::: ai-disclosure-inline
**A note on AI in this research.** This project was an experiment in developing an AI-assisted research methodology. Two senior researchers, Aengus Bridgman and Taylor Owen, designed a pipeline in which AI tools were embedded at each stage of the process, from study design to data collection and response coding to statistical analysis and prose drafting to graphic design, to test what this methodology could produce in a compressed timeline. Claude (Anthropic) was the primary AI tool used throughout. All code and content was reviewed, tested, and verified by Bridgman and/or Owen. The methodology itself is part of what this project set out to test.
:::

:::

::: {.why-it-matters-fr}
## Pourquoi c'est important

\vspace{6pt}

Le journalisme canadien fait partie de l'infrastructure démocratique. Il est essentiel à la reddition de comptes, au débat public et à la participation civique éclairée. Le journalisme est aussi coûteux à produire et sous de fortes pressions financières depuis deux décennies. Les entreprises d'IA en extraient désormais la valeur à chaque étape : en ingérant les archives de presse comme données d'entraînement, en produisant du contenu dérivé sans nommer les sources, et en livrant des réponses aux consommateurs qui peuvent réduire le besoin et l'incitatif de consulter la source originale.

\vspace{6pt}

Ce n'est pas une préoccupation hypothétique. Nos données montrent que les modèles d'IA possèdent une connaissance détaillée de l'actualité canadienne, une connaissance qui, pour la majorité des sujets testés, provient fort probablement du journalisme canadien. Ils répondent à des questions sur la politique, les politiques publiques et la société canadiennes avec une précision qui désigne le journalisme canadien comme source principale. Ils incluent souvent des liens vers des sites de médias canadiens, mais nomment rarement les journalistes ou les organisations dont les reportages alimentent la réponse. Un consommateur qui ne clique pas sur chaque lien ne saura pas quel journalisme il vient de consulter. Le résultat est un système dans lequel les entreprises d'IA monétisent le savoir des journalistes tandis que les salles de rédaction qui l'ont produit reçoivent des liens mais pas de reconnaissance. Cette dynamique, si elle se maintient à grande échelle, risque d'accélérer le déclin même du journalisme dont ces modèles dépendent.

\vspace{6pt}

Une note de politique complémentaire, « AI News Audit: AI, Canadian Journalism, and Paths for Policy Action », examine les implications de ces résultats pour le droit d'auteur canadien, la Loi sur les nouvelles en ligne, les licences légales et les normes d'attribution.

::: ai-disclosure-inline
**Note sur l'utilisation de l'IA dans cette recherche.** Ce projet constituait une expérimentation en méthodologie de recherche assistée par l'IA. Deux chercheurs principaux, Aengus Bridgman et Taylor Owen, ont conçu un processus dans lequel des outils d'IA ont été intégrés à chaque étape — de la conception de l'étude à la collecte de données, du codage des réponses à l'analyse statistique, de la rédaction à la conception graphique — afin de tester ce que cette méthodologie pouvait produire dans un délai réduit. Claude (Anthropic) a été le principal outil d'IA utilisé tout au long du projet. L'ensemble du code et du contenu a été révisé, testé et vérifié par Bridgman et/ou Owen. La méthodologie elle-même fait partie de ce que ce projet cherchait à évaluer.
:::

:::

:::

\clearpage
# Context {.context}

The relationship between technology companies and news organizations has been contentious for over a decade. Social media platforms extracted advertising revenue while distributing news content, prompting regulatory responses including Canada's Online News Act (C-18) and Australia's News Media Bargaining Code.^1^ AI companies present a different challenge: they ingest, process, and deliver journalists' work directly to consumers, often without visible attribution. Major AI companies have signed licensing deals with U.S. and U.K. publishers, but no Canadian news organization has reached a comparable agreement — the major Canadian publishers are suing instead.^2^ The value transfer is asymmetric: journalists produce the knowledge that AI models draw on, but AI companies may capture the traffic and revenue that journalism needs to survive.

AI models interact with Canadian journalism at three distinct stages, each involving a different form of value extraction. At the **ingestion** stage, AI companies scrape and train on Canadian news archives, embedding journalists' knowledge into model weights (the internal parameters that encode everything a model has learned). At the **production** stage, AI models synthesize that journalism into substitute products: answers that deliver much of the reporting's value directly to consumers, with links to news sites but rarely naming the originating outlet." At the **distribution** stage, AI models become the channel through which the public encounters news, but rarely send readers back to the source, diverting the traffic that sustains journalism.

Each stage maps to a distinct policy question. Copyright law governs ingestion, and Canada's fair dealing doctrine under the Copyright Act has never been applied to large-scale AI training.^3^ Derivative works and competition law govern production: the creation of substitute goods from copyrighted material.^4^ Bargaining codes and platform regulation govern distribution, but C-18 was designed for platforms that link to news, not models that synthesize and deliver journalism's value in a fundamentally different way.^5^ The empirical question must come before the policy question: are AI models actually doing this? At what scale? With what consequences for which news organizations?

Against this backdrop, we designed an empirical audit of four leading AI companies and their use of Canadian journalism. This brief traces the three stages of that value chain — ingestion, production, and distribution.

### Questions

**1. Have AI models absorbed Canadian journalism?** We test whether the back catalogue of Canadian news is already embedded in AI training data (the text a model was trained on before release) by asking models about real events from the past three years without web access, particularly stories about domestic politics, provincial affairs, and local events that receive little or no international coverage.

**2. Do AI models produce journalism's value without its costs?** When models draw on Canadian reporting to answer consumer questions, do they deliver something that substitutes for the original article? We measure how often AI responses cover enough distinctive reporting to reduce a consumer's need to visit the source, and whether paywalls provide any meaningful protection.

**3. Do consumers get sent back to the source?** AI models are becoming a primary channel through which the public encounters news. We test whether consumers have any pathway back to the original reporting, and which outlets get visibility when models do cite sources.

Figure `r fig_num("methodology")` summarizes the research design. Track 1 tested for knowledge of events from headlines derived from `r format(n_stories_total, big.mark = ",")` Canadian news stories (English and French) using economy and flagship models from four AI companies — ChatGPT (OpenAI), Claude (Anthropic), Gemini (Google), and Grok (xAI) — with web search disabled, so any knowledge the models show must come from their training data. We use each company's disclosed training cutoff (the date after which no new information was included in the model's training): stories before the cutoff could have been ingested during training, while stories after it serve as a control. Track 2 enables web search and asks models about `r n_articles` specific February 2026 articles from both free and paywalled Canadian outlets, measuring whether the resulting responses substitute for the original journalism and whether Canadian news outlets are mentioned and/or users are directed back to the source. We coded each response using a combination of rule-based checks and LLM-based assessment (see Methodology for full details).

![Figure `r fig_ref("methodology")`: Methodology overview: two empirical tracks testing how AI models interact with Canadian journalism.](images/methodology_simple.png){.figure .inplace}


# Analysis {.questions}

## 1) Have AI models absorbed Canadian journalism? {.question}

The first question is foundational: have AI companies ingested Canadian journalism into their training data? To test this, we prompted economy and flagship models from all four AI companies on `r format(n_stories_total, big.mark = ",")` real Canadian news stories (English and French) between January 2024 and February 2026 **without enabling web search**. Disabling web access means any knowledge the models show must come from their training data. Each model received a brief headline-style description of a real story and was asked what it knew. We then coded whether the response showed accurate knowledge of the event and whether it attributed that knowledge to any source. Stories before a model's training cutoff could have been ingested during training; stories after it could not. If a model knows pre-cutoff stories but not post-cutoff ones, the knowledge almost certainly comes from training data — and for these overwhelmingly domestic stories (Canadian politics, provincial policy, local events), Canadian journalism is the most plausible primary source.

```{r fig2-compute-accuracy, cache=TRUE, dependson="load-data"}
# Shared prompts metadata for joining story dates
prompts_meta <- t1_prompts |> select(story_id, date)

compute_accuracy <- function(data, cutoff_map, tier_label) {
  data |>
    mutate(
      story_date = as.Date(date),
      agent_cutoff = cutoff_map[as.character(agent_clean)],
      period = if_else(story_date <= agent_cutoff, "Pre-training", "Post-training"),
      period = factor(period, levels = c("Pre-training", "Post-training")),
      accurate = knowledge_level %in% c("knowledgeable", "partial") &
                 accuracy %in% c("accurate", "mostly_accurate")
    ) |>
    group_by(agent_clean, period) |>
    summarise(pct_accurate = mean(accurate) * 100, n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(tier = tier_label)
}

fig2_cheap    <- compute_accuracy(bind_rows(t1,      t1_fr),      cutoff_lookup,          "Economy")
fig2_flagship <- compute_accuracy(bind_rows(t1_flag, t1_fr_flag), flagship_cutoff_lookup, "Flagship")

fig2_data <- bind_rows(fig2_cheap, fig2_flagship) |>
  mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Economy", "Flagship")))

# Wide format for segments
fig2_wide <- fig2_data |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = tier, values_from = c(pct_accurate, n),
              names_sep = "_") |>
  mutate(
    improve = pct_accurate_Flagship - pct_accurate_Economy
  )

# Inline stats
fig2_pre <- fig2_wide |> filter(period == "Pre-training")
fig2_post <- fig2_wide |> filter(period == "Post-training")
avg_pre_cheap <- mean(fig2_pre$pct_accurate_Economy)
avg_pre_flagship <- mean(fig2_pre$pct_accurate_Flagship)
avg_post_cheap <- mean(fig2_post$pct_accurate_Economy)
avg_post_flagship <- mean(fig2_post$pct_accurate_Flagship)
avg_improve_pre <- mean(fig2_pre$improve)
avg_improve_post <- mean(fig2_post$improve)

# Per-model knowledge rate (knowledgeable+partial, regardless of accuracy)
# Note: avg_pre_cheap/flagship above are the stricter joint condition (knowledgeable+partial AND accurate)
compute_knowledge <- function(data, cutoff_map, tier_label) {
  data |>
    mutate(
      story_date   = as.Date(date),
      agent_cutoff = cutoff_map[as.character(agent_clean)],
      in_training  = story_date <= agent_cutoff,
      knows        = knowledge_level %in% c("knowledgeable", "partial")
    ) |>
    filter(in_training) |>
    group_by(agent_clean) |>
    summarise(pct_knows = mean(knows) * 100, .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(tier = tier_label)
}

fig2_knowledge <- bind_rows(
  compute_knowledge(bind_rows(t1, t1_fr),           cutoff_lookup,          "Economy"),
  compute_knowledge(bind_rows(t1_flag, t1_fr_flag), flagship_cutoff_lookup, "Flagship")
)

avg_knows_cheap    <- mean(fig2_knowledge$pct_knows[fig2_knowledge$tier == "Economy"])
avg_knows_flagship <- mean(fig2_knowledge$pct_knows[fig2_knowledge$tier == "Flagship"])

# Claude economy knowledge rate (notable outlier — high refusal rate)
claude_knows_econ <- fig2_knowledge |>
  filter(tier == "Economy", agent_clean == "Claude") |>
  pull(pct_knows)

# Economy knowledge rates by model (for footnote)
economy_knows <- fig2_knowledge |>
  filter(tier == "Economy") |>
  select(agent_clean, pct_knows) |>
  deframe()

# ── T-tests: economy vs flagship accuracy ──
# Compute per-story accuracy for each tier, then test
t1_accuracy_by_story <- function(data, cutoff_map, tier_label) {
  data |>
    mutate(
      story_date = as.Date(date),
      agent_cutoff = cutoff_map[as.character(agent_clean)],
      period = if_else(story_date <= agent_cutoff, "Pre-training", "Post-training"),
      accurate = as.integer(knowledge_level %in% c("knowledgeable", "partial") &
                            accuracy %in% c("accurate", "mostly_accurate")),
      tier = tier_label
    ) |>
    select(story_id, agent_clean, period, accurate, tier)
}

t1_stories_cheap    <- t1_accuracy_by_story(bind_rows(t1,      t1_fr),      cutoff_lookup,          "Economy")
t1_stories_flagship <- t1_accuracy_by_story(bind_rows(t1_flag, t1_fr_flag), flagship_cutoff_lookup, "Flagship")
t1_stories_both <- bind_rows(t1_stories_cheap, t1_stories_flagship)

# Pre-training t-test
tt_pre <- t.test(accurate ~ tier,
                 data = t1_stories_both |> filter(period == "Pre-training"))
tt_pre_t <- round(abs(tt_pre$statistic), 2)
tt_pre_p <- format.pval(tt_pre$p.value, digits = 2, eps = 0.001)
tt_pre_diff <- round(diff(tt_pre$estimate) * 100, 1)  # Flagship - Economy

# Post-training t-test
tt_post <- t.test(accurate ~ tier,
                  data = t1_stories_both |> filter(period == "Post-training"))
tt_post_t <- round(abs(tt_post$statistic), 2)
tt_post_p <- format.pval(tt_post$p.value, digits = 2, eps = 0.001)
tt_post_diff <- round(diff(tt_post$estimate) * 100, 1)

# Holm-corrected p-values for the two tier-comparison tests
tt_adj <- p.adjust(c(tt_pre$p.value, tt_post$p.value), method = "holm")
tt_pre_p_adj <- format.pval(tt_adj[1], digits = 2, eps = 0.001)
tt_post_p_adj <- format.pval(tt_adj[2], digits = 2, eps = 0.001)
```

```{r fig2-knowledge-accuracy, fig.width=7.1, fig.height=4.2, cache=TRUE, dependson="fig2-compute-accuracy"}
library(ggrepel)

# Reshape for slope chart: facet by tier, x = period, y = accuracy
fig2_tier <- fig2_data |>
  mutate(
    tier = recode(tier, "Economy" = "Economy models", "Flagship" = "Flagship models"),
    tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Economy models", "Flagship models")),
    period_label = if_else(period == "Pre-training",
                           str_wrap("Canadian journalism plausibly in training data", 20),
                           str_wrap("Post-training period", 20)),
    period_label = factor(period_label, levels = c(
      str_wrap("Canadian journalism plausibly in training data", 20),
      str_wrap("Post-training period", 20)))
  )

# Horizontal nudge: consistent per-agent offset (not rank-based)
logo_nudge <- 0.08
agent_nudge <- setNames((seq_along(agent_levels) - 2.5) * logo_nudge, agent_levels)
fig2_tier <- fig2_tier |>
  mutate(x_nudge = agent_nudge[as.character(agent_clean)])

# Label data — split for left/right nudging
fig2_labels_pre <- fig2_tier |> filter(period == "Pre-training") |>
  mutate(label = paste0(round(pct_accurate), "%"))
fig2_labels_post <- fig2_tier |> filter(period == "Post-training") |>
  mutate(label = paste0(round(pct_accurate), "%"))

# Logo data for geom_image as data points (both periods)
logo_tbl <- tibble(
  agent_clean = factor(agent_levels, levels = agent_levels),
  image = agent_logos[agent_levels]
)
fig2_logos <- fig2_tier |>
  left_join(logo_tbl, by = "agent_clean")

# Build segment data: connect pre to post logo positions with nudge
# Lines should start/end at logo edges, not centers
logo_half_w <- 0.06  # approximate half-width of logo in x-axis units
fig2_segments <- fig2_tier |>
  select(agent_clean, tier, period, pct_accurate, x_nudge) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = period, values_from = c(pct_accurate, x_nudge),
              names_sep = "_") |>
  rename(y_pre = `pct_accurate_Pre-training`, y_post = `pct_accurate_Post-training`,
         nudge_pre = `x_nudge_Pre-training`, nudge_post = `x_nudge_Post-training`) |>
  mutate(
    x_pre  = 1 + nudge_pre + logo_half_w,   # start at right edge of pre logo
    x_post = 2 + nudge_post - logo_half_w   # end at left edge of post logo
  )

p2 <- ggplot(fig2_tier, aes(x = period_label, y = pct_accurate,
                              group = agent_clean, color = agent_clean)) +
  # Segments from logo edge to logo edge
  geom_segment(data = fig2_segments,
               aes(x = x_pre, xend = x_post, y = y_pre, yend = y_post,
                   color = agent_clean, group = agent_clean),
               linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.7, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  # Logos as data points — nudged horizontally
  geom_image(data = fig2_logos,
             aes(x = as.numeric(period_label) + x_nudge,
                 y = pct_accurate, image = image),
             inherit.aes = FALSE, size = 0.05, asp = 1.5) +
  # Left labels
  geom_text_repel(data = fig2_labels_pre,
                  aes(x = as.numeric(period_label) + x_nudge, label = label),
                  size = 3, fontface = "bold", family = "poppins",
                  direction = "y", nudge_x = -0.3,
                  hjust = 1, segment.size = 0.2, segment.color = "grey70",
                  min.segment.length = 0.2, box.padding = 0.3,
                  seed = 42, show.legend = FALSE) +
  # Right labels
  geom_text_repel(data = fig2_labels_post,
                  aes(x = as.numeric(period_label) + x_nudge, label = label),
                  size = 3, fontface = "bold", family = "poppins",
                  direction = "y", nudge_x = 0.3,
                  hjust = 0, segment.size = 0.2, segment.color = "grey70",
                  min.segment.length = 0.2, box.padding = 0.3,
                  seed = 42, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ tier, ncol = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = agent_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0.5, 2.5), breaks = c(1,2), labels = c(str_wrap("Canadian journalism plausibly in training data", 20), str_wrap("Post-training period", 15))) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(-5, 80),
    breaks = seq(0, 80, 20),
    labels = label_percent(scale = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL, y = "% of stories accurately recounted"
  ) +
  theme_brief +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold", family = "poppins",
                              hjust = 0.5, color = "#272B26"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(margin = margin(r = 8)),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing = unit(1.5, "lines"),
    plot.margin = margin(5, 30, 10, 15)
  )

# Agent legend bar (logos + text names, centered across full width)
agent_legend_df <- tibble(
  x = c(1, 2.5, 4, 5.5),
  agent = agent_levels,
  image = agent_logos[agent_levels],
  color = agent_colors[agent_levels]
)
agent_legend <- ggplot(agent_legend_df) +
  geom_image(aes(x = x, y = 0, image = image), size = 0.18, asp = 5) +
  geom_text(aes(x = x + 0.2, y = 0, label = agent, color = agent),
            hjust = 0, size = 2.8, family = "poppins", fontface = "bold",
            show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = agent_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0.3, 6.5)) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))

p2_final <- plot_grid(agent_legend, NULL, p2, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.06, 0.03, 1))
p2_final
save_plot(p2_final, "fig2_knowledge_accuracy", data = fig2_data, width = 7.1, height = 4.5)
```

![Figure `r fig_num("knowledge")`: Accuracy of AI model responses to Canadian news stories, by training data cutoff. Economy and flagship models queried without web search. N = `r format(nrow(t1) + nrow(t1_flag) + nrow(t1_fr) + nrow(t1_fr_flag), big.mark = ",")` responses across `r format(n_distinct(t1$story_id) + n_distinct(t1_fr$story_id), big.mark = ",")` stories (English and French).](analysis/figures/fig2_knowledge_accuracy.png){.figure}

Figure `r fig_ref("knowledge")` shows the results. Note that our corpus draws exclusively from national outlets; local and regional coverage was not tested. The pattern is consistent across model tiers. Models showed at least partial knowledge of pre-cutoff Canadian news events in `r round(avg_knows_cheap)`% of economy-tier responses and `r round(avg_knows_flagship)`% of flagship-tier responses, then dropped sharply for post-cutoff stories (`r round(avg_post_cheap)`% and `r round(avg_post_flagship)`% accurate respectively). Among knowledgeable responses, economy models were accurate `r round(avg_pre_cheap)`% of the time on average; flagship models `r round(avg_pre_flagship)`%. Flagship models score higher overall (unsurprisingly, given they typically have larger training datasets) but the decline after the training cutoff appears across all models and tiers. This points to training data as the source of the knowledge rather than general reasoning, and suggests that more capable models have absorbed more Canadian news content, not less. The specific sources (Canadian journalism, wire services, Wikipedia, or other publicly available text covering the same events) cannot be isolated from model behavior alone.^[Economy-tier knowledge rates vary substantially by model: ChatGPT (`r round(economy_knows["ChatGPT"])`%), Gemini (`r round(economy_knows["Gemini"])`%), and Grok (`r round(economy_knows["Grok"])`%) all demonstrated knowledge in the large majority of pre-cutoff responses, while Claude demonstrated knowledge in only `r round(claude_knows_econ)`% — not because it lacked training data, but because it defaults to refusing rather than guessing when uncertain. As a result, Claude's low rate pulls down the economy-tier average, causing it to understate the knowledge the other three models actually demonstrate. Two-sample t-tests comparing per-response accuracy between economy and flagship models: pre-training stories t = `r tt_pre_t`, p `r tt_pre_p`; post-training stories t = `r tt_post_t`, p `r tt_post_p`. Flagship models were `r abs(tt_pre_diff)` percentage points `r if_else(tt_pre_diff > 0, "more", "less")` accurate on pre-training stories and `r abs(tt_post_diff)` percentage points `r if_else(tt_post_diff > 0, "more", "less")` accurate on post-training stories. Note that each model has a different training data cutoff date, so the pre- and post-cutoff story pools differ in size and date range across models and tiers; the averages reported here are means of per-model percentages.] ChatGPT and Grok show higher post-cutoff accuracy than Claude or Gemini, but this reflects hallucination — generating confident-sounding but fabricated information — not knowledge. Inspection of the responses reveals that these models confidently generate plausible-sounding but fabricated accounts of events they cannot have seen in training data: inventing specific but wrong outcomes (e.g., stating the Bank of Canada *held* rates when it actually *cut* them), producing generic descriptions vague enough to be unfalsifiable, or fabricating events wholesale. Claude, by contrast, typically acknowledges ignorance, resulting in lower measured "accuracy" but more honest responses.^[For post-cutoff stories, `r hall("ChatGPT","econ","pct_claims")`% of ChatGPT's economy-tier responses addressed the topic substantively enough to be coded as showing at least partial knowledge — despite the model never having seen the stories in training. Of those, `r hall("ChatGPT","econ","pct_wrong")`% were coded as inaccurate: the model generated confident, detailed responses about the right topic but fabricated the specifics (e.g., when asked about Calgary's June 2024 water crisis caused by a catastrophic water main break, it invented a scenario involving flash flooding and overwhelmed storm infrastructure). Grok's economy responses were similar: `r hall("Grok","econ","pct_claims")`% addressed post-cutoff stories substantively, with `r hall("Grok","econ","pct_wrong")`% of those coded inaccurate. Claude generated substantive responses for only `r hall("Claude","econ","pct_claims")`% of post-cutoff stories, declining to answer the rest. Flagship models are substantially less prone to this behaviour: ChatGPT's flagship responses were coded as showing knowledge of only `r hall("ChatGPT","flag","pct_claims")`% of post-cutoff stories (vs. `r hall("ChatGPT","econ","pct_claims")`% economy) and Grok's flagship `r hall("Grok","flag","pct_claims")`% (vs. `r hall("Grok","econ","pct_claims")`%), suggesting that hallucination is concentrated in cheaper, smaller models. This means the post-cutoff "accuracy" figures in Figure `r fig_ref("knowledge")` likely overstate true knowledge for economy-tier ChatGPT and Grok, which hallucinate more freely, and understate the relative honesty of models like Claude that refuse to guess.]

Despite showing substantial knowledge of Canadian current events, models rarely attributed it. Models showed at least partial knowledge in `r round(pct_knowing_training)`% of responses to pre-cutoff stories. Among those responses, `r round(pct_no_cite_knowing)`% provided no source attribution of any kind — no named outlet, no recommendation to check a specific news organization, no general reference to "Canadian media." The training cutoff dates used here are self-reported by each company and may be approximate; some information could also reach models through reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) — a process where human evaluators help fine-tune model behavior after initial training — system prompt updates, or other post-training processes. The sharp accuracy drop visible in Figure `r fig_ref("knowledge")` at each model's reported cutoff is consistent with these dates being broadly accurate.

\vspace{1em}

## 2) Do AI models produce journalism's value without its costs? {.question}

AI models have absorbed Canadian journalism at scale and almost never attribute it, even for stories within their training window. We tested directly what happens when models can actively search the web. We enabled web search and asked models about `r n_articles` real articles from February 2026. Figure `r fig_ref("reproduction")` shows the default consumer experience: what happens when someone asks a generic topic question without requesting citations. This is how most people use AI models: "Tell me about X," not "What did the Toronto Star report about X?"

With web search enabled, models retrieve content from across the web, including Canadian news sites, and synthesize it into responses. The blue squares show how often the result covers enough of the article's distinctive reporting (specific events, named individuals, key findings) that a reader could plausibly get the gist of the story without visiting the news site. These are not complete reproductions: they are partial summaries and paraphrases that cover some of the original article's distinctive content, though they sometimes contain factual errors or omissions (see "A note on accuracy" below). We evaluated each response against the source article to determine whether it covered the article's distinctive reporting, not merely the general topic.^[The "covers distinctive reporting" measure combines three LLM-coded levels: partial coverage (`r pct_partial`% of all generic-unprompted responses), close paraphrase (`r pct_close`%), and verbatim reproduction (`r pct_verbatim`%). Partial coverage — where the response covers article-specific events and findings in paraphrased form rather than reproducing exact facts or language — accounts for the majority of viable substitutes. Rule-based pattern-matching alone captures only a fraction of this semantic coverage, which is why LLM-based assessment was necessary. Even partial coverage of a story's distinctive reporting can reduce the consumer's need to visit the source, but readers should note that the blue category reflects "covers some of the article-specific reporting," not full reproduction.] The green squares show how often the model credits the source by naming the outlet in the response text or via structured machine-readable citations returned alongside the response.

Coverage rates are high while attribution rates are not. Gemini and Claude covered distinctive reporting in `r round(pw_generic_repro["Gemini"])`% and `r round(pw_generic_repro["Claude"])`% of responses respectively, but Gemini credited the source only `r round(pw_generic_names["Gemini"])`% of the time. Grok covered distinctive reporting in `r round(pw_generic_repro["Grok"])`% of responses while citing the source in only `r round(pw_generic_names["Grok"])`% of them. ChatGPT, one of the most widely used models, covered distinctive content in `r round(pw_generic_repro["ChatGPT"])`% of responses but almost never credited the originating newsroom. Even when models fail to cover the distinctive reporting, they still deliver a topical response that can reduce the consumer's motivation to visit the source. AI models use web search to retrieve current journalism and deliver it directly to consumers, rarely crediting the newsroom that produced it.^[A pilot sample of `r n_t2_flagship_pilot` flagship-tier responses showed no significant differences on any tested outcome (all p > 0.05; see Methodology). This is expected: substitution and attribution behaviour are driven by each provider's search and citation architecture.]

![Figure `r fig_num("reproduction")`: Coverage of distinctive reporting and source attribution for recent Canadian news articles. Economy models with web search enabled under generic framing with no citation prompt. N = `r n_generic_unprompted` responses across `r n_distinct(t2_url$article_id)` articles.](analysis/figures/fig3_reproduction_vs_attribution.png){.figure}


```{r fig3-waffle, fig.width=7.1, fig.height=3.6, cache=TRUE, dependson="load-data"}
# ── Compute per-agent stats (generic, unprompted) ──
fig3_stats <- t2_url |>
  filter(framing_label == "Generic", citation_label == "Unprompted") |>
  mutate(
    reproduces = llm_reproduction %in% c("partial", "close_paraphrase", "verbatim")
  ) |>
  group_by(agent_clean) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    pct_reproduces = round(mean(reproduces) * 100),
    pct_cites = round(mean(cited) * 100),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# ── Build waffle grid (10x10 per agent) ──
build_waffle <- function(agent_name, pct_reproduces, pct_cites) {
  cats <- c(
    rep("Credits the source", pct_cites),
    rep("Covers, no credit", pct_reproduces - pct_cites),
    rep("Generic topic response", 100 - pct_reproduces)
  )
  tibble(
    agent_clean = agent_name,
    cell = 1:100,
    col = ((cell - 1) %% 10) + 1,
    row = ((cell - 1) %/% 10) + 1,
    category = factor(cats, levels = c(
      "Credits the source", "Covers, no credit", "Generic topic response"
    ))
  )
}

waffle_data <- pmap_dfr(fig3_stats, function(agent_clean, n, pct_reproduces, pct_cites) {
  build_waffle(as.character(agent_clean), pct_reproduces, pct_cites)
}) |>
  mutate(agent_clean = factor(agent_clean, levels = agent_levels))

waffle_colors <- c(
  "Credits the source"    = "#467742",
  "Covers, no credit"     = "#6BADC6",
  "Generic topic response" = "#E8E6E3"
)

# ── Summary labels ──
fig3_labels <- fig3_stats |>
  mutate(
    covers_label = paste0(pct_reproduces, "% cover content"),
    credit_label = paste0(pct_cites, "% credit source")
  )

# ── Per-agent alpha ──
agent_alpha <- c("ChatGPT" = 0.85, "Claude" = 0.95, "Gemini" = 1.0, "Grok" = 0.90)

make_waffle_panel <- function(agent_name) {
  d <- waffle_data |> filter(agent_clean == agent_name)
  sl <- fig3_labels |> filter(agent_clean == agent_name)
  ld <- tibble(image = agent_logos[agent_name], x = 3.5, y = 13.2)
  a <- agent_alpha[agent_name]

  p <- ggplot(d, aes(x = col, y = row, fill = category)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1, width = 0.92, height = 0.92, alpha = a) +
    geom_image(data = ld, aes(x = x, y = y, image = image),
               inherit.aes = FALSE, size = 0.12, asp = 1.3) +
    annotate("text", x = 6.2, y = 13.2, label = agent_name,
             size = 2.8, family = "poppins", fontface = "bold",
             color = agent_colors[agent_name], hjust = 0) +
    annotate("text", x = 5.5, y = -0.5, label = sl$covers_label,
             size = 2.4, family = "poppins", fontface = "bold", color = "#6BADC6") +
    annotate("text", x = 5.5, y = -1.5, label = sl$credit_label,
             size = 2.4, family = "poppins", fontface = "bold", color = "#467742") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = waffle_colors) +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 11.2), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-1.8, 14.2), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    coord_equal(clip = "off") +
    theme_void() +
    theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 2, 0, 2))

  p <- p + guides(fill = "none")
  p
}

panels <- map(agent_levels, make_waffle_panel)

# ── Shared legend (built as a standalone ggplot) ──
legend_df <- tibble(
  x     = c(1, 3.2, 5.6),
  label = c("Credits the source", "Covers, no credit", "Generic topic response"),
  color = c("#467742", "#6BADC6", "#E8E6E3")
)
shared_legend <- ggplot(legend_df) +
  geom_point(aes(x = x, y = 0), shape = 15, size = 4, color = legend_df$color) +
  geom_text(aes(x = x + 0.15, y = 0, label = label), hjust = 0,
            size = 2.8, family = "poppins", colour = "#272B26") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0.5, 8.5)) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(-30, 0, 0, 0))

grid2 <- plot_grid(plotlist = panels, nrow = 1, align = "h")

p3 <- plot_grid(grid2, shared_legend, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(1, 0.05))
p3
save_plot(p3, "fig3_reproduction_vs_attribution", data = waffle_data, width = 7.1, height = 3.6)
```

This substitution extends to paywalled content. We tested whether paywalls provide any protection across seven outlets spanning the spectrum: free (CBC News, Radio-Canada, Canadian Press), general paywalled (Toronto Star, Montreal Gazette, National Post), and niche paywalled (The Logic). Averaged across all paywalled outlets, models covered distinctive reporting from originally paywalled content (`r round(pw_paid_repro_pct)`%) at rates comparable to free outlets (`r round(pw_free_repro_pct)`%). The competitive news market ensures the same information appears elsewhere — stories are syndicated through wire services, reposted on aggregators, and shared across social media. Paywalls hold only when an outlet's journalism is distinctive enough that no free or more easily accessible substitute exists; competitive coverage renders most paywalls irrelevant.

For most stories, paywalls are bypassed rather than breached: the competitive news market ensures the same information appears across multiple outlets, syndicated through wire services, reposted on aggregators, and shared on social media. However, API citation logs from some models showed direct citations to paywalled URLs, including hard-paywalled niche outlets, with extensive verbatim reproduction of the source text. This suggests that some models' web retrieval tools may access paywalled content that would be blocked for human readers. Whether the mechanism is direct access or competitive syndication, the result is the same: paywalled journalism is reproduced without compensation.

**A note on accuracy.** Track 1 shows a clear pattern of hallucination for post-cutoff stories: models generate plausible-sounding narratives that match the general topic but get key facts wrong.
